caption: In diretta da Milano con Jacinda Ardern e Kati Piri.
text: Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. E poi la gioia di ospitare qui una donna che è stata per noi grande ispirazione mentre era prima ministra del suo paese, la Nuova Zelanda, e ha ispirato le progressiste e i progressisti oltre ogni confine. Jacinda Ardern, grazie di essere con noi. Accoglietele con tutto il calore di cui siete capaci. Grazie. Grazie. Grazie. Grazie a tutti. Grazie perché siete voi il cuore pulsante della nostra comunità. Senza la base, scordiamoci le altezze. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. Grazie a tutti. e empatia. E do you think it is enough to be a female leader to embody a different kind of leadership? Just asking for a friend. Yes, yes. I mean, my question to anyone, to any audience, let alone an audience perhaps of like-minded people, would be what did you teach and value when it came to raising your children? Or when it comes to being a role model to others, what do we value? No, we value curiosity. We value bravery and courage and generosity. And we also value kindness. We teach our children to be kind. Why, when it comes to political leadership, do we interpret kindness to be weak? In the world we live in today, the decision to act with empathy is an act of courage and bravery. It takes strength to be in politics. It takes even more strength to be an empathetic leader. So I believe these values are not just values that are held or should be held by women. We should expect them of all of our leaders. You founded a fellowship field to strengthen the network of empathetic leaders and connect women that are engaged in politics all across the world. Can you tell us a little bit more about it? Do you know, I think that in politics right now we have this very singular focus on a particular style of leadership. And I think it's one of the reasons that we see increasingly people are disengaging from politics. And we see in the global surveys there's a survey that's been happening internationally for many years, the Edelman survey. It tracks trust and confidence in politics and it has found that not only have we got very low levels of trust in politics right now we have a sense of grievance. People feel a grievance towards our political leaders because they feel that disconnection. And we can see why. The type of politics and leadership they see daily in the media is often in the extremes. People who are using inflammatory statements of blame to try and attract voters and to also keep the spotlight away from them making a difference in people's lives. And so the reason that I'm working on empathetic leadership in part is to model that an alternative is not only viable it can be successful but also because I want to put a spotlight on those who are in politics to serve people not to serve themselves. And there are good people still in politics. Two of them are on this stage here. I don't want people to lose hope. Cathy, in the Netherlands we have seen the failure of the extreme rights of Gert Wilders to form a government that was chaos for months in your country and then to keep it in charge for more than a year. Now you're facing this very important national election in the Netherlands on the 29th of October so what is at stake for your country? Thank you and buonasera a tutti. That's all you will hear in Italian from me. Grazie. Yes, it's going to be important elections. Of course every election is always important but I have the feeling that there's a lot at stake at these national elections in the Netherlands. We had for the first time the most far-right government ruling the Netherlands since the Second World War and to be very honest for most people this meant that everything stood still. There were four parties including Gert Wilders as the biggest party trying to govern the Netherlands. It was chaos every week and they did not manage with big promises they made to the electorate they did not manage to do anything on the promises they made. They didn't manage to put any law into legislation. So actually to be honest Ellie for a lot of people time stood still and if you're rich it's fine if time stands still but if you need good education if you want something to be done with climate change adaptation if you want something to be done with housing one of the biggest problems in the Netherlands is that young people cannot they're still living with their parents they cannot move to an affordable housing you cannot allow your country to stand still for years so that's one of the biggest issue another issue which is at stake is democracy itself we have seen when the far right is in power they don't respect parliament they don't always respect how things were done for many years they try to do emergency legislation bypassing parliament and of course the blame politics that is the selling point of the far right putting people up against another and perhaps as a last point one of the saddest thing is that you know a lot of right wing parties said Geert Wilders became the biggest let him govern his voters will see he doesn't deliver and then he will be less popular the opposite is true the opposite is true actually right now you only have one force on the right which is the far right it's only the far right of people in politics in the Netherlands is now down to 4% 4% so is the trust of politics being able to make a change in people's life that's what's at stake at this election and I very much hope that all the democratic parties will take this very seriously and show that a different type of politics is possible a way of politics where we can standing still Jacinda you have been the leader of the laborist party in New Zealand and were elected prime minister for six years of mandate what are the policies you managed to implement that you are most proud of and at the same time what is instead a difficult choice or moment that you remember during your time in charge I mean I remember very early on in my political career I to give a minute silence because a past member of parliament had passed away and the first few times that happened I knew the people because I loved politics but I remember the first time I didn't know the name of is with your name on it it's about the policies that stick and to make a policy stick people have to believe in it they have to cherish it they have to love it they have to make sure that no one takes it away from them and sometimes it means building consensus for those policies so the things I am proud of are some of the things that we did on climate change that we managed to get the opposition to vote for I'm proud that we managed to ban military style semi-automatic weapons in New Zealand and that the opposition voted for it I'm proud that we passed child poverty laws and that the opposition voted for it I'm proud of the things that I that you managed to set a high ambition on fighting climate change we remember that we remember you passing very strong climate legislation we were taking inspiration out of it I remember that I was in a campaign for Emilia-Romagna region at the time and we were saying look we have the same population quite verification to check out if it was true that we were the same and we could do the same as New Zealand but how would you explain something that also me and Cathy have been working on for years when we were together in the European Parliament that social justice and climate justice are deeply interconnected how would you explain that with complete examples also from what you have done in New Zealand I debate in my country in my country we are not having a debate about whether or not climate change is real because we are deeply climate affected and our neighbours are deeply climate affected we are a Pacific nation one of the realm countries of New Zealand is a set of three atolls called Tokelau I visited one of those atolls and I stood at the edge of the atoll as the leader of that atoll showed me a graveyard where they had buried their people that New Zealand had helped build a sea wall to protect the gravesite and only after a few years it was gone their atoll was washing away and when you think about it from that perspective from the perspective of those where this is not a debate about what will happen in 50 years time it's happening now Tuvalu is trying to get the international community to allow them to still be defined as a nation state if they don't have land because Tuvalu will be one of the first islands to go so in my country we absolutely understand that if you don't have a home you literally don't have anymore because the weather is so changeable that no one will flood protect you or landslides increase or your crops are lost climate change affects people's lives and livelihoods so the debate we're having is how fast do you go and how much responsibility do you hold now as a progressive my view is always a collective one no one should be off the hook we all have responsibility there is no geographic boundaries when it comes to this issue because what you do here affects us at the bottom of and a historic congress where that sealed the merge la fusione of your labor party with the growing links can you tell us what brought your parties to that decision and how did it change your political vision of the future yeah it was a nice congress look I think it started already 20 25 years ago that members of our parties were talking with each other especially when we're in opposition like perhaps we should do a merger but then every time that the labor party went into government there was no such interest anymore for merging with the greens who were in the opposition and I think if you look you know Ellie and me we were in the European Parliament and who were the allies of labor always in the European Parliament when it came to whether it's fighting for climate change but also whether it's fighting for raising the minimum wage for affordable housing for all those social justice issues our natural allies have the Greens and Labour Party were always the ones competing with each other for votes we were always fighting each other for votes in the Netherlands we have 15 political parties in Parliament and the last Parliament even had 19 parties in Parliament and this is where four years ago a couple of us said it's enough it's enough the moment that for the European Parliament elections they published 30 questions which each party had to fill in and the Greens separately from the Labour we all filled in the same answers to 30 questions so they had to make up a 31st question to difference between the two parties when it comes to policies and this is the reality it's always you're fighting for the same votes rather than uniting there are so many issues which we have in common and the fight we need to do today is so much bigger than this bickering on the left and it's always the leadership which making sure that left parties unite ellie is i think the example of how you try to align against the far right in italy all the left wing progressive voters and the funny thing is that the first time we had a vote of our members whether they want these two parties to merge into one above 90% of the members said yes and every vote we had since then was above 90% people want to have a united left a united left which is fighting for their low for their livelihoods and this is where after the far right won the last national elections we had hope the end of october we will also show at the national level that united we stand strong whether it's in italy or whether it's in the netherlands this is what we should be doing bravo i couldn't agree more kathy and you challenge was to put together different political cultures that now are feeding us the new generation who keep together all the different political culture the socialist the catholic democratic culture but also the communist culture the democratic culture so we and it's working but i think that unity is important also for another matter and we are happy to be able to say and to welcome you today in the festa democratica di milano la festa dell'unità by saying that we managed in the last weeks to build the same progressive coalition in all the seven regions that will go to vote and it hasn't happened for 20 years because we believe in unity to fight the rights and what they are doing to our country Jacinda talking about party organization in your book you describe one moment after the primaries for the leadership of the party where your candidate lost by all the leaks that had destabilized the party over so many years calmed down asking again for a friend how did you do it yeah and just to say that again I spent nine years nine long years waiting to do something useful in restless and they get frustrated and that's when people are restless and frustrated and waiting to be useful and to make change sometimes they start fighting amongst themselves and sometimes that leads to leaks and destabilization and the undermining of leaders when that starts to happen I have one question that I ask is this why are you here if you are here because you like to play politics if you are here because you like the cut and thrust and you like the games then you're in the wrong place and people will see it the public will see that you are there for the art of politics and not to serve them more that you will bring yourself down if you fight amongst yourselves so always ask the question why are you here and if it's not for the people then move on in your book you also is that too blunt is that too blunt in your book you often talk also about the so called imposter syndrome this is the idea of imposter syndrome yes does that translate yes and this is something women experience in all different roles and jobs we have how did you learn to live with it while bearing the responsibility of ruling your country you know I think the main point you make there is how did you learn to live with it people often ask me how did you overcome it and I don't know that you do you know if you have ever doubted your ability to do a job or wondered if you're the right person then that's a version of imposter syndrome and I because anyone they'll do something differently they'll prepare they'll research they'll anticipate risk they'll bring in an expert or an advisor or someone who could make sure that they have all the information they need to make the best decision they will bring humility to leadership and maybe that's what we need a little bit more of in these times so I think my position changed I don't see imposter syndrome as a weakness anymore I think actually it's a strength so that's the only advice I can offer Cathy when we met in July there was let's say a tough debate tough debate around your stance your position on Gaza when you rightly proposed an embargo to stop selling weapons to Netanyahu's government what was the impact of that position and did it put pressure to change the position of the right wing government yes I think we all are witnessing one of the biggest war crimes that we can see on a daily basis on our phones when we entered the television when we watched the news and there is no leadership standing up and saying no and this is enough and this is a problem we are facing all over the world all over the world so when we organized our furious and they were attacking us for days before a party congress but how can anyone explain to any country to any country that we would be sending weapons to a country committing genocide to a country committing ethnic cleansing we would never do that with any country so this double standard why we are doing that with Israel is the double standard of the right it's the double standard of the right and I think you see it all over not only in Europe I think also the people in the country are much more critical than the politicians I have never in the 11 years that I'm in politics I have never seen a topic where there's such a gap between what the people expect from their politicians to do and what politicians are doing so yes we had just like here in Italy I there's a red line that we are not willing to cross and we had the same in Holland we had 200,000 people on So that's why according to us the minimum the minimum we should be doing is a full weapon embargo stop trade with illegal settlements recognize the state of Palestine and make sure that those people who are responsible are being brought to justice and perhaps Ellie if I can add one sentence because it was a tough debate in my party and we had some members who left the hands we had and I fully understand whatever your personal position is especially if you're a Jewish Dutch how difficult it can be sometimes that people are blaming you for what Israel is doing so we had more or less 800 people who left the party after that congress but we also gained 20,000 new people after that party congress who said this is the movement that I want to belong to and we are now the biggest political movement in the Netherlands and let me add to that that in these days there is probably the biggest humanitarian mission starting spontaneity from the bottom that is leaving for Gaza the global sumo flotilia and we stand in solidarity with the activists and we want the European governments and the European Union to protect them because they were threatened and we have to say that what they are doing is trying to break the illegal block of humanitarian aid and food to Palestinians but they are only doing what the governments of the European Union were supposed to do and we are proud that among thousands of activists there will be four members of the Italian parliament including two members of Partito Democratico Arturo Scotto and Annalisa Corrado and we say Buon Vento because they represent all of and let me also add that we are fed up with the hypocritical words of our government while they vote against the shy sanctions that the European Commission has proposed for the Israeli government we will continue as we did in the big rally in Rome in June with 300,000 people we will continue to ask sanctions for the crimes in Gaza and in the West Bank by the government of Netanyahu and for rich people like in the terrible videos in the social media of Donald Trump that's why we will insist for the full recognition of a state for Palestinians because they have the same right as Israelis to exist and to live in freedom and security in their own state Jacinda your leadership gives us hope and enthusiasm and your time as prime minister inspired lots of women and girls beyond any border including myself but we are in times where the international network of nationalists seem to be winning in many countries with the return of wars and the criminal war also Gaza in the other wars in Sudan and the attack to multilateralism by the extreme right and the attack to fundamental rights of people and communities what is the way forward for us progressives to push another vision for the future and to beat them two really important things I think that as progressives we need to remember the first is I hear this all the time you know the populist movements they're winning you know the far right they're winning what is winning what is winning because yes you might hold office you might increase the number of seats that you hold but if along the way an increasing number of people become disenfranchised stop showing up to vote stop believing in your institutions their grievances grow and their belief in acts of violence as a way to make change increase then where does that leave our societies fractured it leaves our democracies unstable no one wins as a result of that kind of politics so what is winning it has to be more than just taking power it has to only to model what is important about the way we do politics but also what we do when we have power we have to answer the call that people are leaving with us to respond to them Kati is right Ali is right that sense of grievance and disenfranchisement is because the distance between what politicians are doing and what the public are asking for is growing people may say that what impact does a war in the Middle East have on your daily lives well if people look that that war is not ending then they believe that humanity and politics is gone and when they believe humanity and politics is gone they stop voting they stop believing in democracy and they become indifferent and we have seen what a world where people are looks like so as progressives I think we need to remember what is winning and who are we here to serve when we are in office we must remind people that there is still humanity in politics a good place to start is ending the war in Gaza the next place we go to must be improving people's daily lives remind people reading your book and I suggest to all of you to do it but I don't want to spoiler anything but there is one moment in which you spoiler alert I became prime minister and then I quit so yeah but when you become prime minister you're in the car going to the swearing in I think and you talk to a journalist and he asked you what do you want to do and you have all the set of policies in mind of what you will be working for years to do on social issues on child poverty on climate but you give a different answer and you say bringing back kindness so my question would should bringing back kindness be a consistent part of our political program while facing and competing with these shouting opponents in other words because it's the same question we hear in Italy sometimes what would you answer to those who say that in order to win we should shout like them well just because people are used to seeing that does not mean it's the kind of system that we are going to be a very good politician and so I went in believing that I would never be a leader nor did I believe that I had the right skills to be a leader because of the kind of leadership I had seen before that's what people will believe a politician is until someone shows them that it's not until someone like you or someone like you is in a leadership position and shows people that you can be a politician and you can value kindness so to both of you I want to thank you for role modeling that I want to thank you for showing people that you can do politics differently despite years of doing it modeling so as we would say in New Zealand kia kaha kia kaha means keep going stay strong keep doing what you're doing and you'll be making a huge difference to a huge number of people we had a good teacher then Kathy how important it forces across the borders as progressives because they're doing it and they're effective the rhetoric of hatred of Trump Netanyahu Orban Meloni Salvini is strengthening each other with the same enemies they need enemies every day so that they don't have to answer to the fact that they're not delivering for the daily problems of our citizens think about minimum wage think about health care for example but how important it is for us to learn to stick together and to show that we share the same vision I remember when we first met we went together to Lampedusa to show that coming coming from different backgrounds but we were sticking together and that was powerful as we were together in fields as we were together in Utrecht and as we will be again at the congress of the party of the European Socialists that will take place in Amsterdam in October before the election so how important it all welcome to come on the 19th of October to the PS congress in Amsterdam so happy to see you there it will be less sunny though I think when it fled Hungary in communist times because he didn't have any opportunities to study there he didn't have any opportunities to develop there so he was 16 when he fled through Austria to the Netherlands so for me it was always key that democracy is not something to be great taken for granted and that you need international solidarity in to beat also repressive regimes and this is where for me when talking like I told you we have 15 parties in Holland six of them are on the left or seven of them are on the left but there's only one party one party which from the moment that we the Dutch labor party and not one of the other parties because we need international solidarity whether it's for workers rights the tradition of the movement is from workers right whether we look at today's challenges when it comes to climate change when it much more on its own confidence more than we are doing now to be honest so I'm worried about today's developments when it comes to Europe and like Ellie keeps always keep saying why do we know all those names of all the leaders she just mentioned and we know much less names of the progressive leaders that are for Jacinda also for having started and bringing together leaders from European countries working together exchanging that's why I'm very grateful that Ellie although I know she's very busy in Italy also made time to come to the Netherlands and to speak also at the Dutch Congress where she is that we are fighting together we usually say Cathy in the meeting of the party of European socialists that we must not leave internationalism to the nationalists because we have much more in common for example the fight that we're doing now across all Europe with our members of the European parliament on housing because housing is a fundamental right of people and the left has forgotten it for many years at least in this country now we have put it to follow a path to I'm very lucky that in New Zealand I was the third female prime minister and that when I left office we reached the milestone that 50% of our members of parliament were women but just because we had the numbers did not mean the experience was improving in fact I think it is getting harder globally for women in politics and there are a lot of reasons for that there's just really one thing though amongst those many issues that I would want to highlight my day-to-day experience in politics was still a joyful one yes there were extra experiences that I had that others may not have because I was a woman in politics yes that is true undeniably but I would hate for anyone out there listening to think that that meant that they should not put themselves forward if my daughter came to me tomorrow and asked me that she wants to go into politics I and I'll be right by your side because there is no job in the world that is as privileged and as humbling as being able to be a political representative and change the issues that you see in day-to-day lives on behalf of others and so yes we have problems to fix we have problems to fix do not let that stop you from going into we need more of you and just know that when you do choose to run that there will be other women there beside you to support you in ways that perhaps we didn't have in the past thank you jacinda thank you katie thank you for being here it was a beautiful gift for us let me just finish in italian if you allow me grazie a tutte grazie a tutti per la vostra partecipazione è stato per me un regalo bellissimo avere qui jacinda avere qui kati due donne il cui impegno continua a ispirare me e tante tanti altri ogni giorno tra i progressisti europei io vi ringrazio anche per l'impegno unitario che stiamo mettendo in tutta italia vi ringrazio perché questa testardaggine unitaria ha permesso lo dicevo prima di chiudere la stessa alleanza progressista in tutte e sette le regioni che sono al voto siamo al fianco dei nostri candidati siamo al fianco del partito democratico ci siamo con le nostre priorità che sono chiare la difesa della sanità pubblica universalistica dai tagli e dalla privatizzazione di questa destra che qui in Lombardia conoscete molto bene non una sanità per ricchi non una sanità a misura del portafoglio ma una sanità universalistica come quella di Tina Anselmi una sanità che curi chi da solo non ce la fa la difesa della scuola pubblica dai tagli di 6.000 insegnanti la difesa della scuola pubblica che vuol dire anche moltiplicare gli asili nido in tutto il territorio nazionale il lavoro dignitoso a partire dalla battaglia che continuerà contro la precarietà e per un salario minimo perché sotto i 9 euro allora non è lavoro è sfruttamento non deve essere legale e accanto al lavoro le politiche industriali che Giorgia Meloni ha completamente dimenticato tutti gli imprenditori che incontriamo qui al nord come al sud ieri in Calabria accanto a Pasquale Tridico come prima grande questione ci pongono il costo dell'energia abbiamo le bollette più care di Europa non lo dobbiamo accettare possiamo fare come la Spagna possiamo fare come il Portogallo possiamo separare il costo dell'energia da quello del gas che è la fonte spiro alle famiglie italiane e alle imprese italiane per il rilancio della nostra economia se Giorgia Meloni in tre anni non l'ha fatto è perché forse non ha il coraggio di intaccare gli extra profitti maturati da un pugno di grandi società energetiche a scapito di tutte le altre imprese e le altre famiglie italiane andiamo avanti lavoro politiche industriali diritto alla salute diritto alla scuola e diritti diritti sociali e civili che per noi sono inscindibili sono inscindibili come il diritto alla casa sono inscindibili come il diritto a supportare per davvero le famiglie non con la retorica vuota di chi poi taglia i fondi per i nidi non protegge l'assegno unico per cui tanto ci siamo battuti diritto di tutte le famiglie perché loro non fanno che parlare di vuota retorica sulla famiglia come se fosse una sola tradizionale di cui tanto parlano ma che poi nessuno di loro ha suggerirei un po' di cautela le famiglie sono tante Milano lo sa sono plurali e vanno tutte difese nel contributo che portano alle nostre comunità nessuna discriminata e soprattutto non si possono discriminare i loro figli e le loro figlie per cui continueremo a batterci accanto ai nostri sindaci e amministratori che ringrazio per il coraggio anche accanto alle figlie e figli delle coppie omogenitoriali come difenderemo tutte le altre famiglie italiane andiamo avanti così con valori che guardano al futuro radicati nel passato e nella nostra meravigliosa costituzione che come tutte e tutti noi è orgogliosamente antifascista grazie a tutte grazie a tutti e viva Jacinda Ardner e viva Catipiri e viva le feste dell'unità e viva il partito democratico forza Milano e viva l'Italia antifascista grazie grazie
